full_notes:
Towns Traders and Craftspersons is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that 61 DEVOTIONAL PATHS TO THE DIVINE You may have seen people perform rituals of worship, or singing , or , or even repeating the name of God in silence, and noticed that some of them are moved to tears.

The text explains that such intense devotion or love of God is the legacy of various since the eighth century.

The text explains that the Idea of a Supreme God Before large kingdoms emerged, different groups of people worshipped their own gods and goddesses.

The text explains that as people were brought together through the growth of towns, trade and empires, new ideas began to develop.

The text explains that the idea that all living things pass through countless cycles of birth and rebirth performing good deeds and bad came to be widely accepted.

The text explains that similarly, the idea that all human beings are not equal even at birth gained ground during this period.

The text explains that the belief that social privileges came from birth in a “noble” family or a “high” caste was the subject of many learned texts.

The text explains that many people were uneasy with such ideas and turned to the teachings of the Buddha or the Jainas according to which it was possible to overcome social differences and break the cycle of rebirth through personal effort.

The text explains that others felt attracted to the idea of a Supreme God who could deliver humans from such bondage if approached with devotion (or bhakti).

The text explains that this idea, advocated in the grew in popularity in the early centuries of the Common Era.

The text explains that dEVOTIONAL PATHS TO THE DIVINE The beginning of Bhakti Worship of certain deities, which became a central feature of later Hinduism, gained in importance.

The text explains that these deities included Shiva, Vishnu, and goddesses such as Durga.

The text explains that these deities were worshipped through Bhakti, an idea that became very popular at this time.

The text explains that bhakti is generally understood as a person’s devotion to his or her chosen deity.

The text explains that anybody, whether rich or poor, belonging to the so-called ‘high’ or ‘low’ castes, man or woman, could follow the path of Bhakti.

The text explains that the idea of Bhakti is present in the Bhagavad Gita, a sacred book of the Hindus.

The text explains that in the process, local myths and legends became a part of the Puranic stories, and methods of worship recommended in the Puranas were introduced into the local cults.

The text explains that you can observe this process of local myths and legends receiving wider acceptance even today.

The text explains that today we use these texts as sources for writing histories of the bhakti tradition.

The text explains that as the famous Gujarati saint Narsi Mehta said, “They are Vaishnavas who understand the pain of others.” Questioning the social order This is an abhang (Marathi devotional hymn) of Sant Tukaram: with the battered and the beaten Mark him as a saint For God is with him He holds Every forsaken man Close to his heart He treats A slave As his own son Says Tuka I won’t be tired to repeat again Such a man Is God In person.

The text explains that this process was st re ngthened with t he est ab lis hment of th e Delhi Sultanate (Chapter 3), when several major Sufi centres developed all over the subcontinent.

The text explains that new Religious Developments in North India The period after the thirteenth century saw a new wave of the bhakti movement in North India.

The text explains that we saw that new kingdoms (Chapters 2, 3 and 4) were emerging, and people were taking up new people, especially craftspersons, peasants, traders and labourers, thronged to listen to these new saints and spread their ideas.

The text explains that a unique feature of most of the saints is that their works were composed in regional languages and could be sung.

The text explains that his teachings openly ridiculed all forms of external worship of both Brahmanical Hinduism and Islam, the pre-eminence of the priestly classes and the caste system.

The text explains that he himself used the terms , and for the essence of his teaching, which actually meant right worship, welfare of others and purity of conduct.

The text explains that his teachings are now remembered as , and , which also underline the importance of right belief and worship, honest living, and helping others.

The text explains that this might partly explain the difference between the history of the followers of Baba Guru Nanak and the history of the followers of like Kabir, Ravidas and Dadu, whose ideas were very similar to those of Baba Guru Nanak.

The text explains that match the following: The Buddha Shankaradeva worship of Vishnu Nizamuddin Auliya questioned social differences Alvars worship of Shiva Imagine You are attending a meeting where a saint is discussing the caste system.

The text explains that there are several saint-poets whose names have been mentioned but their works have not been included in the chapter.

The text explains that why do you think many teachers rejected prevalent religious beliefs and practices?

The text explains that what were the major teachings of Baba Guru Nanak?

The text explains that for either the Virashaivas or the of Maharashtra, discuss their attitude towards caste.

The text explains that why do you think ordinary people preserved the memory of Mirabai?

The text explains that find out whether in your neighbourhood there are any or temples associated with saints of the bhakti tradition in your neighbourhood.

The text explains that visit any one of these and describe what you see and hear.

The text explains that for any of the saint-poets whose compositions have their works, noting down other poems.

The text explains that find out whether these are sung, how they are sung, and what the poets wrote about.

The text explains that find out more about the language in which they composed, whether their compositions were sung, and what their compositions were about.

Source: NCERT Class 7 Our Pasts II, Chapter 6: Towns Traders and Craftspersons
